original_hypothesis.title: transferToUnoccupiedPlot Missing plotId Update Permanently Bricks Landlord Plots
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: transferToUnoccupiedPlot updates plotOccupied for old and new plots but never updates toilerState[tokenId].plotId. When the renter subsequently unstakes, unstakeMunchable reads the stale plotId from toilerState and clears the OLD plot (already empty), leaving the NEW plot permanently marked as occupied with no way to clear it. Any renter can grief any landlord by calling transferToUnoccupiedPlot followed by unstakeMunchable, permanently bricking one plot per operation.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a confirmed missing state update with a clear exploitation path. The toilerState.plotId remains stale after transfer, creating a bidirectional desync between plotOccupied and toilerState. The permanently occupied plot can never be used again since there is no admin function to clear plotOccupied.

proof_of_signal.pattern: plotOccupied[landlord][oldPlotId] and plotOccupied[landlord][plotId] are updated but toilerState[tokenId].plotId is never set to the new plotId

preconditions: Renter has at least one staked munchable
There exists an unoccupied plot on the same landlord to transfer to

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: toilerState[tokenId].plotId is never assigned the new plotId in transferToUnoccupiedPlot
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call transferToUnoccupiedPlot(tokenId, newPlotId) then unstakeMunchable(tokenId). unstakeMunchable reads stale _toiler.plotId = oldPlotId, clears already-empty slot, leaves newPlotId permanently occupied.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack: each operation permanently locks one landlord plot. Repeated calls can brick ALL of a landlord's plots, destroying their rental income and locking future stakers out.

economic_questions: How many plots can a renter brick per transaction cycle?
Is there any admin mechanism to clear plotOccupied that could mitigate this?
What is the total economic impact of bricking a landlord's entire plot capacity?
validation_requirements: Confirm toilerState[tokenId].plotId is never updated in transferToUnoccupiedPlot
Verify no admin or recovery function can reset plotOccupied
Test the full stake → transfer → unstake sequence to confirm permanent lock

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Only gas costs + one munchable NFT (reusable across attacks)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.001 ETH per plot bricked (gas for stake + transfer + unstake)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; pure griefing destroying landlord's rental capacity
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A (griefing attack, economic damage to landlord unbounded)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Permanent destruction of landlord's plot capacity; affects all current and future renters

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Targeted landlord (all plots can be bricked), future stakers on those plots
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Permanent loss of rental income for landlord; stakers locked out of plots
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All landlord plot capacity in the protocol

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: No parameters needed; code path is deterministic
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace state through stake → transfer → unstake

validation_result.impact_summary: transferToUnoccupiedPlot never updates toilerState[tokenId].plotId, causing unstakeMunchable to clear the wrong (already-empty) plot and permanently bricking the new plot as occupied forever.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The transferToUnoccupiedPlot function at LandManager.sol lines ~171-197 updates plotOccupied for both the old and new plots and updates toilerState[tokenId].latestTaxRate, but critically never assigns toilerState[tokenId].plotId = plotId. After the transfer, toilerState[tokenId].plotId still points to the old plot. When unstakeMunchable is subsequently called, it reads _toiler.plotId (the stale old value) and clears plotOccupied[_toiler.landlord][_toiler.plotId] — which is the old plot that was already cleared by transferToUnoccupiedPlot. The new plot at plotOccupied[landlord][newPlotId] remains permanently marked as {occupied: true, tokenId: tokenId} with no code path that can ever clear it. There is no admin function, no emergency function, and no other mechanism in the contract to reset plotOccupied entries.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code inspection confirms the missing assignment. In transferToUnoccupiedPlot, only toilerState[tokenId].latestTaxRate is written (line ~186). The plotId field is never touched. Compare with stakeMunchable (lines ~143-153) which correctly sets toilerState[tokenId].plotId = plotId. The unstakeMunchable function (lines ~157-170) reads _toiler.plotId to determine which plotOccupied entry to clear — confirming the desync leads to clearing the wrong entry.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: toilerState[tokenId].plotId is never set to the new plotId. Only latestTaxRate is updated. This leaves toilerState.plotId pointing to the old plot while plotOccupied reflects the new plot, creating a permanent desync.

unstakeMunchable reads _toiler.plotId to determine which plotOccupied slot to clear. After transferToUnoccupiedPlot, _toiler.plotId is stale (points to old plot), so it clears the already-empty old plot and leaves the new plot permanently occupied.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls stakeMunchable(landlord, tokenId, plotId=0) → toilerState[tokenId].plotId = 0, plotOccupied[landlord][0] = {true, tokenId}
2. User calls transferToUnoccupiedPlot(tokenId, plotId=3) → plotOccupied[landlord][0] = {false, 0}, plotOccupied[landlord][3] = {true, tokenId}, but toilerState[tokenId].plotId remains 0
3. User calls unstakeMunchable(tokenId) → reads _toiler.plotId = 0, clears plotOccupied[landlord][0] (already empty), plotOccupied[landlord][3] stays {true, tokenId} forever
4. Plot 3 is now permanently bricked — occupied=true with no way to clear it
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: In transferToUnoccupiedPlot (line ~186), only toilerState[tokenId].latestTaxRate is written. The line `toilerState[tokenId].plotId = plotId;` is missing entirely. Compare with stakeMunchable (lines ~143-153) where toilerState is fully initialized including plotId. In unstakeMunchable (line ~163), plotOccupied[_toiler.landlord][_toiler.plotId] is cleared using the stale plotId from toilerState. Since the old plot was already cleared by transferToUnoccupiedPlot, this is a no-op on the old slot, while the new slot at the transferred plotId remains permanently marked occupied.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment assumptions relevant to this bug. The defect is a code logic error independent of configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward and requires only 3 transactions: (1) stakeMunchable to stake an NFT on a landlord's plot 0, (2) transferToUnoccupiedPlot to move it to plot 3, (3) unstakeMunchable to unstake. After step 3, plot 3 is permanently bricked. The attacker can repeat this with any available plot on any landlord. Each cycle costs only gas and requires one munchable NFT (which is returned on unstake and can be reused). No special conditions, timing, or capital needed beyond being a registered player with one NFT.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. Any registered player can permanently brick any landlord's plots, one per attack cycle. Repeated attacks can brick ALL plots for a given landlord, permanently destroying their rental capacity. There is no admin recovery function to reset plotOccupied. The only theoretical fix would be a contract upgrade. The attack has near-zero cost (gas only) and the damage is permanent and irreversible.
recommended_mitigations: Add `toilerState[tokenId].plotId = plotId;` in transferToUnoccupiedPlot after updating latestTaxRate
Consider adding an admin function to force-clear plotOccupied entries as an emergency recovery mechanism
Add a bidirectional consistency check invariant that verifies plotOccupied and toilerState are always synchronized
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LandManager with test configuration. 2. Register two accounts (landlord and renter). 3. Landlord locks value for ≥4 plots. 4. Renter stakes NFT on plot 0. 5. Renter calls transferToUnoccupiedPlot(tokenId, 3). 6. Assert toilerState[tokenId].plotId == 0 (stale — confirms bug). 7. Renter calls unstakeMunchable(tokenId). 8. Assert plotOccupied[landlord][3].occupied == true (permanently bricked). 9. Attempt to stake a new NFT on plot 3 — should revert with OccupiedPlotError, confirming permanent lockout.
uncertainties: The exact line numbers depend on formatting; the function boundaries are approximate but the code content is verified from the provided source.
additional_testing_needed: Verify the _farmPlots dirty flag behavior with the stale plotId — does it also cause incorrect dirty flag evaluation?
Test whether the emitted FarmPlotTaken event in transferToUnoccupiedPlot shows the stale plotId (it reads toilerState[tokenId] which has stale plotId)
